cards.title: who

variables.label: what you are building
variables.placeholder: a lead qualifier for sales teams

variables.label: who it is for
variables.placeholder: b2b sales reps at smb companies

variables.label: context
variables.placeholder: sales industry, leads not qualified before calls, hubspot integration, clean admin dashboard

variables.placeholder: an ai tool that scores inbound leads before sales calls

variables.placeholder: b2b sales reps at smb companies

items.title: pressure test your idea
items.content:
You are my product thinking coach named Hameed. I have one product idea I want to pressure test before I build anything. Your job is to interview me, not to give me answers.

Rules you must follow:

Ask me one question at a time and wait for my reply before the next one.

Never invent my buyer, their problem, or their losses. If I do not know something, tell me to go find out from a real person.

Keep your questions short and simple. I am not a technical person.

If any answer I give sounds like a guess, ask me how I know it.

Do not suggest a solution until the very end.

Walk me through these four steps in order:

Step 1, Buyer. Ask me who the buyer is. Push me to name a real person I know, not a customer type. Ask me for the names of three real people from my contacts who have this problem. If I cannot name at least one real person, stop and tell me to fix that first.

Step 2, Intent. Ask me what this person is actually trying to achieve. Help me separate what they want, which is the outcome, from how they do it today, which is just their current method.

Step 3, Problem. Ask me what is getting in their way. Help me phrase it as: this buyer wants this outcome, but this is stopping them.

Step 4, Current way and cost. Ask me how this person solves the problem today. Then ask me what this old way is already costing them in time, money, or lost customers. Make me give a real example that has already happened, not one that might happen.

After all four steps, do three things:

One, show me my finished statement in this exact format: Buyer, Intent, Problem.

Two, tell me honestly if this looks like a painkiller, something urgent, or a vitamin, something nice to have.

Three, give me the single most important question I should ask my three real people before I build anything.

Begin now with Step 1, and ask me only the first question.

items.title: best way to build this app
items.content:
Product strategy, MVP scoping, Lovable-first build.

Analyze the best way to build $IDEA for $CUSTOMER.

Context:
$CONTEXT

I am going to vibe code this using Lovable, so the recommendation must be practical for a Lovable-first build.

Choose the single best format from this list:

* Landing page
* Web app
* Mobile app
* Ecommerce marketplace
* Browser extension
* Presentation
* Internal tool
* Personal tool

Output:

Best choice:
[Choose one option only]

Why it works:
[One clear sentence]

Why Lovable fits:
[One clear sentence]

Why not the others:
[One short sentence]

Simplest first version:
[Smallest useful build]

Positioning:
[One sentence to present this to the customer]

items.title: user journey mapping
items.content:
User journey mapping, MVP workflow, admin-first UX.

Create a user journey map for $IDEA built for $CUSTOMER.

Context:
$CONTEXT

Important:

* If this is an internal tool, map only the internal/admin user journey.
* External users should appear only as touchpoints, not app users.
* Keep the journey MVP-friendly.

Output:

1. Core User Journey
   Table columns:
   Stage, User Goal, User Action, System Action, Screen Needed

2. External Touchpoints
   Table columns:
   Touchpoint, Where It Happens, What Happens

3. Simplest Core Flow
   Write as:
   Step 1 → Step 2 → Step 3 → Step 4

4. Screens Required
   Table columns:
   Screen, Purpose, Priority

items.title: feature spec based on stories
items.content:
Product requirements, feature specification, user stories, Lovable MVP spec.

Create a feature specification for $IDEA built for $CUSTOMER.

Context:
$CONTEXT

Output:

1. Feature List
   Table columns:
   Feature ID, Feature Name, Purpose, Priority

2. Detailed Feature Spec
   For each feature, include:

* What it does
* Inputs required
* User actions
* System actions
* Output/result
* Edge cases
* MVP notes

3. User Stories
   Table columns:
   Story ID, Story, Features involved, Remarks

4. MVP Boundary
   Table columns:
   Include in MVP, Push Later, Reason

5. Data Entities
   Table columns:
   Entity, Fields, Notes

6. Integrations
   Table columns:
   Integration, Purpose, Trigger, Result

Rules:

* Keep it Lovable-friendly.
* Avoid overengineering.
* Do not add features outside the core problem.
* Make simple MVP assumptions where needed.

items.title: design.md generation
items.content:
Design system, UI specification, design.md, Lovable-ready visual language.

Create a detailed design.md file for $IDEA built for $CUSTOMER.

Context:
$CONTEXT

The design.md should include:

1. Product UI Direction

* What the product should feel like
* What it should not feel like
* Primary user context

2. Visual Style

* Layout
* Colors
* Typography
* Cards
* Spacing
* Radius
* Shadows
* Icons
* Empty states

3. Design Tokens
   Include:

* colors
* typography
* spacing
* radius
* shadows
* status colors

4. Component System
   Include:

* App shell
* Dashboard cards
* Metric cards
* List cards
* Forms
* Buttons
* Badges
* Tabs
* Filters
* Navigation
* Empty states
* Modals/drawers

5. Screen Layout Guidance
   For each screen:

* Purpose
* Main sections
* Primary actions
* Secondary actions
* Important states

6. Interaction Rules

* Loading states
* Error states
* Success states
* Confirmation states
* Mobile behavior

7. Content Tone

* Button language
* Empty state language
* Alert language
* Reminder language

8. Do / Don't
   Clear rules for future AI agents.

Output only the design.md content.

items.title: coming soon page
items.content:
Landing page copy, coming soon page, waitlist page, MVP validation.

Create a coming soon page for $IDEA built for $CUSTOMER.

Context:
$CONTEXT

The page should include:

1. Hero Section

* Headline
* Subheadline
* Primary CTA
* Secondary trust line

2. Pain Section

* 3–5 pains the customer currently faces

3. Solution Section

* What the product does
* Why it is different
* What outcome it creates

4. Feature Preview

* 5–7 MVP features

5. How It Works

* 3-step flow

6. Who It Is For

* Who it is for
* Who it is not for

7. Early Access CTA

* Form fields
* CTA button copy
* Confirmation message

8. FAQ

* 5 useful questions and answers

9. Final CTA

* Strong closing line

Rules:

* Keep copy direct.
* Do not overhype.
* Do not mention non-MVP features.
* Write for business owners, not technical users.

items.title: generate list of screens
items.content:
Screen architecture, product UX, Lovable app screens, feature-to-screen mapping.

Generate the complete list of screens required for $IDEA built for $CUSTOMER.

Context:
$CONTEXT

Output:

1. Screen List
   Table columns:
   Screen ID, Screen Name, Purpose, Priority

2. Screen Details
   For each screen, include:

* User goal
* Main content
* Primary actions
* Secondary actions
* Empty state
* Error state
* Success state
* Data shown
* Features supported
* Stories supported

3. Navigation Structure
   Show:

* Main navigation
* Secondary navigation
* Detail pages
* Settings pages

4. Feature-to-Screen Mapping
   Table columns:
   Feature, Screen(s), Notes

5. User Story-to-Screen Mapping
   Table columns:
   Story ID, Screen(s), Notes

6. MVP Screen Cut
   Table columns:
   Must Build, Can Delay, Reason

Rules:

* Keep the screen count practical for Lovable.
* Do not create screens for non-MVP features.
* If this is an internal tool, do not create customer-facing screens.

items.title: start prompt with technical keywords / specifications
items.content:
Act as a senior product engineer and Lovable prompt architect.

Create a Lovable build prompt for $IDEA built for $CUSTOMER.

Context:
$CONTEXT

Output:

1. Technical Keywords Block
   Write one comma-separated first line covering:

* app type
* architecture
* UI style
* data model
* authentication
* integrations
* workflows
* dashboard behavior
* responsive behavior
* MVP constraints

2. Lovable Build Prompt
   The prompt must start with the Technical Keywords Block, then include:

* Product context
* User type
* Core workflow
* Screens to build
* Features per screen
* Data entities
* Integrations
* Business rules
* Design style
* Things to avoid

3. Acceptance Checklist
   Table columns:
   Area, Requirement, Pass/Fail Criteria

Rules:

* First line must be technical keywords/specifications.
* Keep it MVP-first.
* Do not include features outside the confirmed scope.

who.title: who
who.eyebrow: take it to the right people

items.title: gtm strategist
items.content:
You are an experienced Go-To-Market Strategist who has launched hundreds of successful SaaS and AI products.

I have built the following product:

$PRODUCT

Target audience (if known):
$AUDIENCE

My goal:
Get my first 10 paying customers.

Your task is to think like a GTM advisor and create a complete GTM strategy.

Follow these steps.

STEP 1 — Understand the Product
Summarize the product in one sentence.

STEP 2 — ICP
Identify the best Ideal Customer Profile.
Include:
- Industry
- Company size
- Decision maker
- Daily responsibilities
- Biggest frustrations
- Current alternatives
- Buying triggers

STEP 3 — Problem Analysis
List:
- Top 10 pain points
- Current ways they solve it
- Why those methods fail
- Cost of not solving it

STEP 4 — Positioning
Generate:
- Value proposition
- Positioning statement
- Elevator pitch
- One-line pitch
- Website headline
- Website subheadline

STEP 5 — Messaging
Create messaging for:
- LinkedIn profile
- Website hero section
- Email introduction
- Cold DM
- WhatsApp message

STEP 6 — Customer Acquisition
Recommend the best acquisition channels.

Rank them.

Explain why.

Include:
- LinkedIn
- Reddit
- Communities
- Facebook Groups
- Events
- Cold Email
- Partnerships
- Referrals

STEP 7 — First 30 Days

Create a day-by-day action plan for getting the first 10 customers.

STEP 8 — Objections

List likely objections.

Generate responses.

STEP 9 — Metrics

What should I measure every week?

STEP 10 — AI Recommendations

Recommend AI tools to automate:
- Content
- Outreach
- Sales
- Follow-up
- Customer Support

Be practical.

Don't give generic startup advice.

Think like a founder with zero marketing team and limited budget.
